text: The surgeon performs a right upper lobectomy with robotic assistance, inserting the assistant port subcostally through the diaphragm. The inferior pulmonary ligament is mobilized, and lymph nodes from station nine or eight are identified in this area.

The surgeon retracts the lung anteriorly to expose the pulmonary vein and bronchus, freeing up the posterior mediastinum. The dissection proceeds in a clockwise fashion from the inferior pulmonary vein and ligament towards the posterior mediastinum, with removal of station seven and subcarinal lymph nodes visible.

The surgeon uses a robotic instrument to grasp and remove lymph nodes located between the upper and lower lobe bronchi. This dissection exposes the posterior ascending branch of the pulmonary artery and opens up the fissure between the lobes.
After removing the lymph node, the surgeon proceeds to the apical part of the mediastinum, targeting station two and station four R lymph nodes. The pulmonary artery is visible to the right.
The surgeon retracts the lung posteriorly and isolates the superior pulmonary vein. The fissure is entered, revealing lymph nodes around the middle lobe artery.
The surgeon uses a robotic green stapler to divide the fissure between the upper and lower lobe, as well as between the upper and middle lobe, with a vessel loop visible through the fissure.

The surgeon uses a robotic white stapler to dissect the posterior ascending branch of the pulmonary artery during a right-sided resection. The surgical field shows the use of a single anterior port where the intercostal space is larger.
The surgeon uses a robotic green stapler to divide the fissure between the upper and middle lobes, following dissection of the anterior mediastinum. This action visibly opens up the fissure.

The surgeon completes the division of the fissure between the upper and middle lobes using a robotic green stapler. The lung is retracted posteriorly, revealing the superior pulmonary vein as the procedure returns focus to the anterior mediastinum.

The surgeon identifies the middle lobe vein and carefully avoids it while maneuvering around the superior pulmonary vein. The superior vein is then addressed, revealing the truncus anterior, which is clearly visualized.

The surgeon uses a vessel loop to elevate the artery, positioning it away from the surgical field. The robotic white stapler is introduced, with its smooth anvil tip carefully maneuvered to avoid damaging nearby vessels. The truncus anterior is divided, revealing the artery and veins in the surgical field.
The surgeon retracts the lung anteriorly, exposing the bronchus to the upper lobe. The robotic green stapler is then introduced through the right hand to engage the bronchus.

The surgeon clamps the bronchus and inflates the lungs to ensure normal inflation of the middle and lower lobes. Following successful inflation, the bronchus is divided, completing the mediastinal lymphadenectomy and the right upper lobectomy.

The surgeon fills the chest cavity with saline to check for air leaks, identifying an air leak in the fissure. Pro-Gel is applied to seal the leak. A twenty French chest tube is positioned anteriorly under direct robotic vision.

Thirty-five percent of our lobectomies go home the next day, and we can't do it if we have an air leak. So we make sure we don't have an air leak when we leave. We then will place the specimen in an anchor bag.

The surgeon uses a 15mm anchor bag to remove the specimen through the assistant port, causing the diaphragm to enlarge and become subcostal, visible through a two-inch incision. The diaphragm is then repaired using a V-lock 2-0 monofilament suture, with the XI robot providing a clear view of the repair process. The tear in the diaphragm is small and follows the direction of the muscle fibers, completing the right upper lobectomy and diaphragm repair.
